title: Basal Cell Carcinoma
oneLiner: Most common skin cancer; locally invasive, rarely metastatic; arises from basal keratinocytes; strongly linked to cumulative UV exposure.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette**  
A 72-year-old fair-skinned retired farmer presents with a slow-growing lesion on his left nasal ala that he first noticed 18 months ago. It has a pearly, translucent border with fine telangiectasias crossing the surface, and a small central ulceration that occasionally bleeds. He is otherwise well, with no prior skin cancer history. On exam, the lesion is firm and non-tender. You think: “This is a classic nodular BCC.”

**Dissecting the vignette**  
- **Fair-skinned, retired farmer**: Chronic UV exposure (occupational risk).  
- **Left nasal ala**: Sun-exposed site (face, ears, neck, scalp – all common).  
- **Slow-growing over 18 months**: Indolent behaviour – BCCs grow slowly, often ignored.  
- **Pearly, translucent border**: Due to nests of basaloid cells with mucin and retraction artefact.  
- **Fine telangiectasias**: Angiogenesis driven by tumour – the “arborizing vessels” seen on dermoscopy.  
- **Central ulceration**: Necrosis from outgrowing blood supply – the “rodent ulcer” phenomenon.  
- **Firm, non-tender**: Malignant nodule; lack of pain helps differentiate from an inflamed cyst or keratoacanthoma.

**Atypical presentations that can trick you**  
1. **Pigmented BCC** – Pearly border may be subtle; the lesion appears darkly pigmented (brown/blue/black) and is easily mistaken for nodular melanoma or a seborrhoeic keratosis. Key clue: pearly component still present at the edge under dermoscopy.  
2. **Morphoeic (sclerosing) BCC** – Presents as a flat, scar-like, indurated plaque with ill-defined borders. It can feel hard like a morphea patch and may be misdiagnosed as a scar or localized scleroderma. Danger: it is more aggressive, infiltrates deeply, and has high recurrence risk if not excised with wide margins.  
3. **Superficial BCC** – A red, scaly, well-defined patch, often on the trunk or limbs. Looks like an eczema or psoriasis patch, but it is non-pruritic and persists for months. Dermoscopy reveals small erosions and “map-like” islands of tumour.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition**  
Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is a skin cancer that starts in the basal layer of the epidermis – the bottom-most layer of the skin. It grows locally and is almost always curable when caught early, but if left untreated it can eat into surrounding tissues (hence “rodent ulcer”), causing significant disfigurement and functional damage.

**Why this matters clinically**  
- **Frequency**: BCC is the most common malignancy in humans – incidence rising with ageing population and sun exposure.  
- **Stakes**: Nearly 100% cure if removed completely. Missed or mismanaged BCCs can lead to extensive local invasion (nose, ear, eye, bone) requiring complex reconstructive surgery.  
- **Common pitfalls**:  
  - Calling a BCC a “pimple” or “benign mole” – watch for persistent, non-healing lesions.  
  - Assuming all BCCs are harmless – morphoeic and infiltrative subtypes can be sneaky.  
  - Forgetting that immunocompromised patients (transplant, HIV, leukaemia) get more aggressive BCCs.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of development**  
UVB radiation (290–320 nm) hits the skin and creates cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers in the DNA of basal keratinocytes. Normally this is repaired, but with cumulative exposure, mutations accumulate – classically in the **PTCH1** gene (part of the Sonic Hedgehog signalling pathway). Loss of PTCH1 function leads to uncontrolled activation of Smoothened (SMO), driving continuous proliferation of basal cells. The tumour grows as islands or cords of basaloid cells in the dermis.

**Why each key clinical sign happens**  
- **Pearly, translucent nodule**: The tumour nests are surrounded by mucinous stroma that causes a milky-white or “pearly” sheen. The epidermis above them is thinned, making the underlying tumour visible.  
- **Telangiectasias**: Tumour secretion of VEGF stimulates new blood vessel formation. These vessels are seen as fine red lines on the surface.  
- **Ulceration**: The central part of the nodule outgrows its blood supply – becomes necrotic and sloughs, leaving a crater (rodent ulcer).  
- **Slow growth**: BCC has a low mitotic rate and is under apoptotic control (though dysregulated). It expands by direct extension, not metastasis.  
- **Scar-like (morphoeic) pattern**: Dense fibrous stroma – the tumour cells are trapped in a sclerotic matrix, making the lesion feel hard and look like a scar.

**Connecting to pattern recognition**  
- The **pearly border** and **telangiectasias** are pathognomonic for nodular BCC – they are direct results of tumour microarchitecture and angiogenesis.  
- **Absence of pain** is because BCC rarely invades nerves early; pain or paraesthesia suggests perineural invasion (a high-risk feature).  
- **Ulceration** indicates a more advanced or neglected lesion – think “rodent ulcer” in the elderly.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Environmental (most common)** | Chronic UV exposure (cumulative sun exposure) | “Retired farmer”, “sailor”, “outdoor worker” |
| **Intermittent intense UV** | Sunburns in childhood/adolescence | “Used to blister as a kid” – more so for BCC than SCC |
| **Immunosuppression** | Organ transplant, HIV, long-term steroids | “On tacrolimus after kidney transplant” |
| **Genetic disorders** | Gorlin syndrome (PTCH1 mutation) | “Multiple BCCs before age 30” + “jaw cysts, palmar pits” |
| **Iatrogenic** | Radiotherapy (e.g., past treatment for acne, cancer) | “Had radiation for Hodgkin’s 20 years ago” – BCC within field |
| **Arsenic exposure** | Contaminated water, Fowler’s solution (historical) | “Lived in Taiwan (blackfoot disease) or Bangladesh” – also palmar/plantar keratoses |
| **Phototherapy (PUVA)** | For psoriasis | “PUVA treatment for years” |

**Most common cause**: Long-term cumulative UV exposure (sunlight).  
**Key point**: BCC is more linked to total lifetime UV dose than to intense intermittent sunburns (which is more strongly associated with melanoma).

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria**  
- Clinical diagnosis is often sufficient for a nodular BCC – but you should always **confirm with histology** before definitive treatment.

**Tests and interpretation**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Dermoscopy** | Arborizing (branching) vessels, blue-grey ovoid nests, ulceration, leaf-like structures (superficial BCC) | First-line non-invasive tool – improves diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity ~90%) | **Single key finding**: Arborizing vessels on a pearly background = BCC until proven otherwise. |
| **Punch/shave biopsy** | Nests of basaloid cells with peripheral palisading, stroma with mucin, artefactual retraction | To confirm diagnosis, assess subtype (e.g., nodular, infiltrative, morrhoeic) | **Confirmatory**: Basaloid nests + palisading. **High-risk**: Perineural invasion, infiltrative growth, morrhoeic pattern (need wider margins). |
| **Excisional biopsy** (if small, suspicious) | Complete excision with margins | Therapeutic + diagnostic – removes the lesion | **Clear margins**: No tumour at specimen edge – no further treatment needed. **Positive margin**: Requires re-excision or radiotherapy. |
| **MRI (rare)** | Deep extension (bone, soft tissue) | Only if high-risk lesion (e.g., periorbital, perineural, recurrent) or clinically fixed | **Feature**: Loss of fat plane, periosteal enhancement, nerve enhancement (perineural spread). |
| **CT (rare)** | Bony destruction | Suspect bone invasion (e.g., large neglected BCC on scalp) | **Key sign**: Erosion of outer table of skull. |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms or rules out**  
- **Confirms**: Histology showing basaloid nests with palisading and retraction artefact.  
- **Rule out**: A lesion that fully heals within 2 weeks is unlikely to be BCC (BCCs don’t self-resolve). Also, if dermoscopy shows no vessels and no pearly border, strongly consider benign (e.g., seborrhoeic keratosis).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour)**  
- **No emergency**. Provide reassurance and schedule appropriate treatment.  
- If the patient is on anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs), plan to continue unless large excision – coordinate with haematology if needed.  
- Counsel patient: this is skin cancer but nearly always curable.

**Ward / outpatient (first 24–72 hours)**  
- **Biopsy** – either shave or punch (for small lesions, excise directly).  
- **Staging** – only if high-risk features: size >2 cm on head/neck, perineural invasion, recurrent disease, immunosuppression. For low-risk nodular BCC, no imaging needed.  
- **Definitive treatment choice** based on subtype, size, location:

| Lesion type | First-line treatment | Common doses / regimen |
|-------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Small, low-risk (face/trunk) | Excision with 4 mm clinical margin | Local anaesthetic, 1% lidocaine with adrenaline, excise to subcutaneous fat |
| High-risk (central face, ear, nose, recurrent) | Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) | Mapped excision, frozen sections until margins clear – no dosing, but requires specialist |
| Superficial BCC (trunk, limb) | Topical imiquimod 5% (or curettage + cautery) | Imiquimod cream: apply 5x/week for 6 weeks to the lesion (2.5 cm² area). Not for nodular or high-risk. |
| Non-surgical candidate (elderly, anticoagulated) | Radiotherapy | 3–6 Gy per fraction, total 30–50 Gy over 2–4 weeks for small lesions (orthovoltage or electron beam) |
| Large/nodular with ulceration | Excision ± flap/graft | Excision with margin 5–10 mm, refer to plastics |
| Recurrent BCC after excision | Re-excision (wider margin) or MMS | 6 mm margin if previous surgery; consider adjuvant radiotherapy |

- **Medical therapy for advanced BCC**: Vismodegib (Smo inhibitor) – 150 mg PO daily for locally advanced or metastatic BCC not amenable to surgery. Requires dermatology-oncology referral.

**Long-term**  
- **Sunscreen education**: SPF 50+ daily, sun avoidance between 10am–4pm, hats, protective clothing.  
- **Total body skin exam** every 6–12 months – BCC patients have ~40% risk of another skin cancer (including SCC and melanoma) within 5 years.  
- **Follow-up**: For high-risk BCC, review at 6 months then annually for life.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**  
- **Don’t** try to “freeze” a large nodular BCC with liquid nitrogen – it will either recur or leave a visible scar; cryotherapy is only for superficial BCCs.  
- **Don’t** reassure a lesion that bleeds and has rolled borders as a “pimple” – biopsy it.  
- **Don’t** discharge the patient without photoprotection counselling – they remain at high risk.  
- **Don’t** perform a shave biopsy on a morphoeic BCC – the scar will obscure the margins, making definitive excision harder.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | BCC | SCC | Melanoma (nodular) | Seborrhoeic keratosis |
|---------|-----|-----|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Pain** | Usually none | Can be tender (especially if ulcerated or perineural invasion) | Usually none; occasional itching | None |
| **Exam** | Pearly, telangiectasias, rolled border, central ulcer | Firm, erythematous nodule or plaque, hyperkeratotic, may have crust | Brown-black irregular nodule with asymmetric shape, irregular borders, can be non-pigmented (amelanotic) | “Stuck-on” waxy plaque, often pigmented, with horn cysts (milia-like) |
| **Dermoscopy** | Arborizing vessels, blue-grey ovoid nests, ulceration | Coiled/serpentine vessels, white circles (keratin pearls), peripheral reddish base | Asymmetric pigmentation, atypical network, blue-black dots (if pigmented); irregular vessels (if amelanotic) | Comedo-like openings, milia-like cysts, fissures (“cerebriform” pattern) |
| **Histology** | Basaloid nests with peripheral palisading, mucinous stroma, retraction artefact | Squamous pearls, intercellular bridges (desmosomes), atypical keratinocytes throughout epidermis | Melanocytes with pagetoid spread, nesting, depth measured by Breslow | Hyperkeratosis, papillomatosis, horn cysts, no atypia |
| **Key differentiator** | Pearly border + telangiectasias | Rapid growth in high-risk patient (e.g., elderly, immunosuppressed; often on lower lip, ear) | Asymmetry, colour variegation, change in a pre-existing mole | Waxy “stuck-on” appearance + horn cysts |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
Each complication tells a story of neglect.

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Local destruction (rodent ulcer)** | Large, fungating, cratered lesion on nose/ear/scalp that gradually erodes cartilage or bone | Visible crater, foul odour, exposure of underlying cartilage or periosteum | Urgent plastics/Mohs surgery + imaging to assess deep extent; may need reconstruction (flap, graft) |
| **Perineural invasion** | Pain, numbness, facial weakness (e.g., VII nerve palsy), or diplopia (III, IV, VI) | Lesion within sensory nerve territory; can be histologically occult – look for nerve thickening on MRI | Wide excision with Mohs + radiotherapy; neurology referral if motor deficit. Prognosis worse. |
| **Periorbital invasion** | Ectropion, entropion, eyelid notch, exophthalmos, diplopia | BCC on lower eyelid or medial canthus – can erode bone into orbit | Oculoplastics referral; CT orbit to check for orbital involvement; exenteration may be needed for advanced cases. |
| **Recurrence** | New bump at or near scar of previous excision | Usually within 3 years; dermoscopy shows new BCC features | Re-excision with wider margin or Mohs; consider radiotherapy second line. |
| **Metastasis (very rare – <0.1%)** | Regional lymph node enlargement (post-auricular, submandibular) or distant organ (lung, bone) | Palpable nodes, systemic symptoms (weight loss, bone pain) | Biopsy node; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis; palliative SMO inhibitor (vismodegib); radiotherapy for painful bone mets. |

**Late-presentation patterns** (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):  
- A 90-year-old woman with a 5-year history of a non-healing sore on her nose presents with a 3 cm ulcer that has destroyed the nasal tip and extended into the columella – **rodent ulcer** with nasal cartilage destruction.  
- A 60-year-old transplant patient has a “pimple” on his right cheek for 2 years that suddenly became painful and caused jaw numbness – his BCC developed **perineural invasion** of the maxillary division of V.  
- A 45-year-old man with Gorlin syndrome has dozens of BCCs; one on his forehead grows to 4 cm and erodes the frontal bone – **bone invasion** requiring craniofacial resection.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**  
- **P.T.R.U.** – Pearly, Telangiectasias, Rolled border, Ulceration (classic nodular BCC).  
- **B.A.C.K.** – Basal cell, Actinically damaged, Chronic sun, Keratinocytes (but here it’s basaloid). Alternative: **BCC = Basal, Clear, Cancer** – just a reminder.  
- **Morphoeic = M.O.R.P.H.** – **M**ore aggressive, **O**ccult margins, **R**ecurrence high, **P**ale scar-like, **H**ard feel.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues)**  
1. **Any non-healing lesion >1 month** in a sun-exposed area should be presumed BCC until proven otherwise.  
2. **Pearl under stretch** – Stretch the skin around a pearly papule; the translucency becomes more obvious (nicknamed “stretch test” – not formal, but useful).  
3. **“Rodent ulcer”** – The classic neglected BCC that progressively eats away the tissue like a rodent gnawing.  
4. **BCCs rarely bleed spontaneously** unless traumatised – if a lesion is steadily bleeding with no trauma, think SCC or other.  
5. **Pigmented BCC** can mimic melanoma – do a dermoscopy: look for blue-grey ovoid nests and arborizing vessels. If unsure, biopsy.  
6. **In young patients (<30) with multiple BCCs, consider Gorlin syndrome** – also look for jaw cysts, palmar pits, calcified falx cerebri.  
7. **Superficial BCC** can be flat and scaly – if it stays in one spot for >6 months and doesn’t respond to steroids, think BCC.

**Common exam/ward mistakes**  
- Biopsying a lesion and only taking the centre (ulcerated part) – may show only granulation tissue. Take the **edge** (the pearly rolled border).  
- Using pure nitrogen cryotherapy on a BCC that has any nodular component – recurrence rate >80%.  
- Failing to measure the lesion before biopsy – you need clinical diameter + margins for surgical planning.  
- Discharging a patient after a single BCC without a full skin check – 40% risk of another lesion (including melanoma) within 5 years.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
When must you escalate? BCC is usually low-risk, but certain features demand specialist referral or urgent action.

**Call a senior / refer to specialist dermatology/plastics when:**

| Criterion | Why? | What to do |
|-----------|------|------------|
| **High-risk site** – central face (eyelids, nose, lips), ear, genitalia, | BCC near critical structures (eye, ear canal) – functional and cosmetic consequences if mismanaged | Refer to Mohs surgeon or specialist surgical dermatologist |
| **Morphoeic/infiltrative subtype** on biopsy | High recurrence rate – 5 mm margins may not be enough; Mohs preferred | Arrange MMS or wide excision with 6–10 mm margin |
| **Perineural invasion** seen histologically | Can cause nerve damage, intracranial spread | Urgent MRI head/neck; refer to head and neck oncology for combined treatment |
| **Lesion size >2 cm on head/neck or >3 cm on trunk/limbs** | Higher risk of incomplete excision and deeper invasion | Discuss with plastics/dermatologist for advanced treatment planning |
| **Recurrent BCC after previous excision** | Scar tissue makes surgical clearance difficult; tumour may be more aggressive | Re-excision with wider margin or Mohs; may need radiotherapy |
| **Immunosuppressed patient** (transplant, HIV, lymphoma) | BCC may be more aggressive and multiple | Multidisciplinary approach; consider dermatology oncology |
| **Suspected metastatic BCC** (palpable nodes, systemic symptoms) | Metastasis is rare but life-changing | Refer to oncology; start vismodegib (Smo inhibitor) |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5 or signs of sepsis** (very rare – only if superinfected ulcer with cellulitis) | Infected BCC can cause systemic infection | IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS), blood cultures, escalate to acute medical team |
| **Any BCC in a child or young adult** | Consider Gorlin syndrome | Genetic counselling + head CT for falx calcification, jaw X-ray for odontogenic keratocysts |

**Specific thresholds** (for BCC, no specific scoring like NEWS2, but use general deterioration):  
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** – if infected BCC with fever, CRP >100, lactate >2 mmol/L, call medical registrar.  
- **GCS < 15** – only if perineural invasion has caused intracranial spread (very rare); needs immediate neurology/neurosurgery consult.

**Last thought**: For the vast majority of BCCs, senior escalation is not needed – you can excise or refer electively. But always err on the side of caution if the lesion is in a high-risk site or you lack the tools to treat it properly (no Mohs availability, no dermatopathologist). The patient’s face is at stake.

## Basal Cell Carcinoma
**One-liner:** Most common skin cancer; locally invasive, rarely metastatic; arises from basal keratinocytes; strongly linked to cumulative UV exposure.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette**  
A 72-year-old fair-skinned retired farmer presents with a slow-growing lesion on his left nasal ala that he first noticed 18 months ago. On exam, the lesion is firm and non-tender. You think: “This is a classic nodular BCC.”

- **Firm, non-tender**: Malignant nodule; lack of pain helps differentiate from an inflamed cyst or keratoacanthoma.

**Atypical presentations that can trick you**  
1. **Pigmented BCC** – Pearly border may be subtle; the lesion appears darkly pigmented (brown/blue/black) and is easily mistaken for nodular melanoma or a seborrhoeic keratosis. Key clue: pearly component still present at the edge under dermoscopy.  
2. **Morphoeic (sclerosing) BCC** – Presents as a flat, scar-like, indurated plaque with ill-defined borders. It can feel hard like a morphea patch and may be misdiagnosed as a scar or localized scleroderma. Danger: it is more aggressive, infiltrates deeply, and has high recurrence risk if not excised with wide margins.  
3. **Superficial BCC** – A red, scaly, well-defined patch, often on the trunk or limbs. Looks like an eczema or psoriasis patch, but it is non-pruritic and persists for months. Dermoscopy reveals small erosions and “map-like” islands of tumour.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

  - Forgetting that immunocompromised patients (transplant, HIV, leukaemia) get more aggressive BCCs.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

- **Ulceration** indicates a more advanced or neglected lesion – think “rodent ulcer” in the elderly.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Environmental (most common)** | Chronic UV exposure (cumulative sun exposure) | “Retired farmer”, “sailor”, “outdoor worker” |
| **Intermittent intense UV** | Sunburns in childhood/adolescence | “Used to blister as a kid” – more so for BCC than SCC |

| **Arsenic exposure** | Contaminated water, Fowler’s solution (historical) | “Lived in Taiwan (blackfoot disease) or Bangladesh” – also palmar/plantar keratoses |

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria**

**Tests and interpretation**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Dermoscopy** | Arborizing (branching) vessels, blue-grey ovoid nests, ulceration, leaf-like structures (superficial BCC) | First-line non-invasive tool – improves diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity ~90%) | **Single key finding**: Arborizing vessels on a pearly background = BCC until proven otherwise. |
| **Punch/shave biopsy** | Nests of basaloid cells with peripheral palisading, stroma with mucin, artefactual retraction | To confirm diagnosis, assess subtype (e.g., nodular, infiltrative, morrhoeic) | **Confirmatory**: Basaloid nests + palisading. **High-risk**: Perineural invasion, infiltrative growth, morrhoeic pattern (need wider margins). |

| **CT (rare)** | Bony destruction | Suspect bone invasion (e.g., large neglected BCC on scalp) | **Key sign**: Erosion of outer table of skull. |

| Superficial BCC (trunk, limb) | Topical imiquimod 5% (or curettage + cautery) | Imiquimod cream: apply 5x/week for 6 weeks to the lesion (2.5 cm² area). Not for nodular or high-risk. |
| Non-surgical candidate (elderly, anticoagulated) | Radiotherapy | 3–6 Gy per fraction, total 30–50 Gy over 2–4 weeks for small lesions (orthovoltage or electron beam) |

- **Medical therapy for advanced BCC**: Vismodegib (Smo inhibitor) – 150 mg PO daily for locally advanced or metastatic BCC not amenable to surgery. Requires dermatology-oncology referral.

**Long-term**  
- **Sunscreen education**: SPF 50+ daily, sun avoidance between 10am–4pm, hats, protective clothing.  
- **Total body skin exam** every 6–12 months – BCC patients have ~40% risk of another skin cancer (including SCC and melanoma) within 5 years.  
- **Follow-up**: For high-risk BCC, review at 6 months then annually for life.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**  
- **Don’t** try to “freeze” a large nodular BCC with liquid nitrogen – it will either recur or leave a visible scar; cryotherapy is only for superficial BCCs.  
- **Don’t** reassure a lesion that bleeds and has rolled borders as a “pimple” – biopsy it.  
- **Don’t** discharge the patient without photoprotection counselling – they remain at high risk.  
- **Don’t** perform a shave biopsy on a morphoeic BCC – the scar will obscure the margins, making definitive excision harder.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | BCC | SCC | Melanoma (nodular) | Seborrhoeic keratosis |
|---------|-----|-----|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Pain** | Usually none | Can be tender (especially if ulcerated or perineural invasion) | Usually none; occasional itching | None |
| **Exam** | Pearly, telangiectasias, rolled border, central ulcer | Firm, erythematous nodule or plaque, hyperkeratotic, may have crust | Brown-black irregular nodule with asymmetric shape, irregular borders, can be non-pigmented (amelanotic) | “Stuck-on” waxy plaque, often pigmented, with horn cysts (milia-like) |
| **Dermoscopy** | Arborizing vessels, blue-grey ovoid nests, ulceration | Coiled/serpentine vessels, white circles (keratin pearls), peripheral reddish base | Asymmetric pigmentation, atypical network, blue-black dots (if pigmented); irregular vessels (if amelanotic) | Comedo-like openings, milia-like cysts, fissures (“cerebriform” pattern) |
| **Histology** | Basaloid nests with peripheral palisading, mucinous stroma, retraction artefact | Squamous pearls, intercellular bridges (desmosomes), atypical keratinocytes throughout epidermis | Melanocytes with pagetoid spread, nesting, depth measured by Breslow | Hyperkeratosis, papillomatosis, horn cysts, no atypia |
| **Key differentiator** | Pearly border + telangiectasias | Rapid growth in high-risk patient (e.g., elderly, immunosuppressed; often on lower lip, ear) | Asymmetry, colour variegation, change in a pre-existing mole | Waxy “stuck-on” appearance + horn cysts |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

Each complication tells a story of neglect.

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Local destruction (rodent ulcer)** | Large, fungating, cratered lesion on nose/ear/scalp that gradually erodes cartilage or bone | Visible crater, foul odour, exposure of underlying cartilage or periosteum | Urgent plastics/Mohs surgery + imaging to assess deep extent; may need reconstruction (flap, graft) |
| **Perineural invasion** | Pain, numbness, facial weakness (e.g., VII nerve palsy), or diplopia (III, IV, VI) | Lesion within sensory nerve territory; can be histologically occult – look for nerve thickening on MRI | Wide excision with Mohs + radiotherapy; neurology referral if motor deficit. Prognosis worse. |
| **Periorbital invasion** | Ectropion, entropion, eyelid notch, exophthalmos, diplopia | BCC on lower eyelid or medial canthus – can erode bone into orbit | Oculoplastics referral; CT orbit to check for orbital involvement; exenteration may be needed for advanced cases. |
| **Recurrence** | New bump at or near scar of previous excision | Usually within 3 years; dermoscopy shows new BCC features | Re-excision with wider margin or Mohs; consider radiotherapy second line. |
| **Metastasis (very rare – <0.1%)** | Regional lymph node enlargement (post-auricular, submandibular) or distant organ (lung, bone) | Palpable nodes, systemic symptoms (weight loss, bone pain) | Biopsy node; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis; palliative SMO inhibitor (vismodegib); radiotherapy for painful bone mets. |

**Late-presentation patterns** (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):  
- A 90-year-old woman with a 5-year history of a non-healing sore on her nose presents with a 3 cm ulcer that has destroyed the nasal tip and extended into the columella – **rodent ulcer** with nasal cartilage destruction.  
- A 60-year-old transplant patient has a “pimple” on his right cheek for 2 years that suddenly became painful and caused jaw numbness – his BCC developed **perineural invasion** of the maxillary division of V.  
- A 45-year-old man with Gorlin syndrome has dozens of BCCs; one on his forehead grows to 4 cm and erodes the frontal bone – **bone invasion** requiring craniofacial resection.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics**  
- **P.T.R.U.** – Pearly, Telangiectasias, Rolled border, Ulceration (classic nodular BCC).  
- **B.A.C.K.** – Basal cell, Actinically damaged, Chronic sun, Keratinocytes (but here it’s basaloid). Alternative: **BCC = Basal, Clear, Cancer** – just a reminder.  
- **Morphoeic = M.O.R.P.H.** – **M**ore aggressive, **O**ccult margins, **R**ecurrence high, **P**ale scar-like, **H**ard feel.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues)**  
1. **Any non-healing lesion >1 month** in a sun-exposed area should be presumed BCC until proven otherwise.  
2. **Pearl under stretch** – Stretch the skin around a pearly papule; the translucency becomes more obvious (nicknamed “stretch test” – not formal, but useful).  
3. **“Rodent ulcer”** – The classic neglected BCC that progressively eats away the tissue like a rodent gnawing.  
4. **BCCs rarely bleed spontaneously** unless traumatised – if a lesion is steadily bleeding with no trauma, think SCC or other.  
5. **Pigmented BCC** can mimic melanoma – do a dermoscopy: look for blue-grey ovoid nests and arborizing vessels. If unsure, biopsy.  
6. **In young patients (<30) with multiple BCCs, consider Gorlin syndrome** – also look for jaw cysts, palmar pits, calcified falx cerebri.  
7. **Superficial BCC** can be flat and scaly – if it stays in one spot for >6 months and doesn’t respond to steroids, think BCC.

**Common exam/ward mistakes**  
- Biopsying a lesion and only taking the centre (ulcerated part) – may show only granulation tissue. Take the **edge** (the pearly rolled border).  
- Using pure nitrogen cryotherapy on a BCC that has any nodular component – recurrence rate >80%.  
- Failing to measure the lesion before biopsy – you need clinical diameter + margins for surgical planning.  
- Discharging a patient after a single BCC without a full skin check – 40% risk of another lesion (including melanoma) within 5 years.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

When must you escalate? BCC is usually low-risk, but certain features demand specialist referral or urgent action.

**Call a senior / refer to specialist dermatology/plastics when:**

| Criterion | Why? | What to do |
|-----------|------|------------|
| **High-risk site** – central face (eyelids, nose, lips), ear, genitalia, | BCC near critical structures (eye, ear canal) – functional and cosmetic consequences if mismanaged | Refer to Mohs surgeon or specialist surgical dermatologist |
| **Morphoeic/infiltrative subtype** on biopsy | High recurrence rate – 5 mm margins may not be enough; Mohs preferred | Arrange MMS or wide excision with 6–10 mm margin |
| **Perineural invasion** seen histologically | Can cause nerve damage, intracranial spread | Urgent MRI head/neck; refer to head and neck oncology for combined treatment |
| **Lesion size >2 cm on head/neck or >3 cm on trunk/limbs** | Higher risk of incomplete excision and deeper invasion | Discuss with plastics/dermatologist for advanced treatment planning |
| **Recurrent BCC after previous excision** | Scar tissue makes surgical clearance difficult; tumour may be more aggressive | Re-excision with wider margin or Mohs; may need radiotherapy |
| **Immunosuppressed patient** (transplant, HIV, lymphoma) | BCC may be more aggressive and multiple | Multidisciplinary approach; consider dermatology oncology |
| **Suspected metastatic BCC** (palpable nodes, systemic symptoms) | Metastasis is rare but life-changing | Refer to oncology; start vismodegib (Smo inhibitor) |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5 or signs of sepsis** (very rare – only if superinfected ulcer with cellulitis) | Infected BCC can cause systemic infection | IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS), blood cultures, escalate to acute medical team |
| **Any BCC in a child or young adult** | Consider Gorlin syndrome | Genetic counselling + head CT for falx calcification, jaw X-ray for odontogenic keratocysts |

**Specific thresholds** (for BCC, no specific scoring like NEWS2, but use general deterioration):  
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** – if infected BCC with fever, CRP >100, lactate >2 mmol/L, call medical registrar.  
- **GCS < 15** – only if perineural invasion has caused intracranial spread (very rare); needs immediate neurology/neurosurgery consult.

**Last thought**: For the vast majority of BCCs, senior escalation is not needed – you can excise or refer electively. But always err on the side of caution if the lesion is in a high-risk site or you lack the tools to treat it properly (no Mohs availability, no dermatopathologist). The patient’s face is at stake.
